1: Now Joseph had been taken down to Egypt. Potiphar, an Egyptian who was one of Pharaoh’s officials, the captain of the guard, bought him from the Ishmaelites who had taken him there — the Joseph narrative resumes after the Judah-Tamar parenthesis. “Taken down to Egypt” (yarad — the same descent-verb) — Joseph’s descent is involuntary, as a slave. The purchased slave who was bought by Ishmael-traders from his own brothers: the betrayal-and-sale by brothers will become the foundational type of Christ’s betrayal. Acts 7:9–10: “Because the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph, they sold him as a slave into Egypt. But God was with him and rescued him from all his troubles.” The descent into Egypt as slave is the type of the incarnation’s descent: “he took the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness” (Philippians 2:7).
2: The LORD was with Joseph so that he prospered, and he lived in the house of his Egyptian master — “The LORD was with Joseph” — the first of four occurrences in this chapter (vv2, 3, 21, 23). The divine presence formula that runs through Genesis: “I am with you and will watch over you wherever you go” (Genesis 28:15, to Jacob). The God who is with the covenant-bearer even in the alien Egyptian household is the God whose presence cannot be contained by geography or circumstance. Christ promises the same: “And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). John 8:29: “The one who sent me is with me; he has not left me alone.” The divine presence that was with Joseph through slavery is the presence that was with Christ through the cross.
3: When his master saw that the LORD was with him and that the LORD gave him success in everything he did — Potiphar, a pagan Egyptian, recognizes that “the LORD was with him.” The covenant God’s presence is visible even to pagans through the covenant-bearer’s life. The Abrahamic blessing flowing to the surrounding world: “all peoples on earth will be blessed through you” (Genesis 12:3). The visible evidence of divine presence that others can observe is the type of the church’s witness: “Let your light shine before others, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven” (Matthew 5:16). Potiphar glorifies not God but his own household’s prosperity — but the recognition is there. The divine blessing through the covenant-bearer is visible to those who look.
4: Joseph found favor in his eyes and became his attendant. Potiphar put him in charge of his household, and he entrusted to his care everything he owned — the slave who rises to steward the household through divine favor. The pattern of the faithful servant who is entrusted with “everything he owned” is the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:21: “you have been faithful with a few things; I will put you in charge of many things”). The slave-steward given charge of everything is the type of Christ as the one to whom the Father has given all authority: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me” (Matthew 28:18); “The Father loves the Son and has placed everything in his hands” (John 3:35). Joseph in Potiphar’s house is the type of Christ in the Father’s household.
5: From the time Potiphar put him in charge of his household and of all that he owned, the LORD blessed the household of the Egyptian because of Joseph — the Egyptian’s household is blessed “because of Joseph.” The covenant-bearer’s presence is the channel of divine blessing to Gentile households — the exact form of the Abrahamic promise: “all peoples on earth will be blessed through you” (Genesis 12:3). The blessing that comes to Potiphar’s household through Joseph’s presence is the type of the blessing that comes to all who receive Christ: “Praise be to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us in the heavenly realms with every spiritual blessing in Christ” (Ephesians 1:3). The blessing is “in Christ” — available to all who, like Potiphar’s household, receive the covenant-bearer.
6: So Potiphar left everything he had in Joseph’s care; with Joseph in charge, he did not concern himself with anything except the food he ate. Now Joseph was well-built and handsome — the stewardship is so complete that Potiphar trusts Joseph with everything. The total entrustment — “did not concern himself with anything except the food he ate” — is the type of the Father’s entrustment of the world to the Son: “The Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son” (John 5:22); “the Father… has placed everything in his hands” (John 3:35). The description of Joseph as “well-built and handsome” — in contrast to the Servant’s description (“no beauty or majesty to attract us to him,” Isaiah 53:2) — shows that Joseph is a type but not an exact copy; the antitype (Christ) will differ at significant points.
7: And after a while his master’s wife took notice of Joseph and said, “Come to bed with me!” — the temptation of the covenant-bearer by the master’s wife. The tempter who uses the language of consent and desire to draw the covenant-bearer into sin is the type of the devil’s temptation of Christ. “Then Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil” (Matthew 4:1). The temptation comes in a context of privilege and success — not in poverty but at the height of Joseph’s stewardship. The devil tempts Christ not in obscurity but after the baptismal affirmation (“This is my Son, whom I love,” Matthew 3:17). Temptation follows affirmation; the enemy attacks when the covenant-bearer is most visibly blessed.
8: But he refused. “With me in charge, my master does not concern himself with anything in the house; everything he owns he has entrusted to my care” — Joseph’s refusal begins with the covenant-faithfulness argument: the trust placed in him makes the betrayal worse, not better. The one entrusted with everything cannot betray the entrustor without destroying the foundation of the relationship. Christ’s faithfulness is precisely this: “Christ is faithful as the Son over God’s house” (Hebrews 3:6); “This is the one God has accredited” (Acts 2:22). The trusted steward who refuses to betray the trust is the type of the Son who is “faithful to the one who appointed him, just as Moses was faithful in all God’s house” (Hebrews 3:2). The refusal of sin on the grounds of faithfulness to the one who trusts.
9: “No one is greater in this house than I am. My master has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. How then could I do this great evil and sin against God?” — Joseph’s refusal reaches its climax: “How then could I do this great evil and sin against God?” The sin is named not primarily as a sin against Potiphar (though it is that) but as a sin against God. The primary orientation of Joseph’s moral life is toward God rather than toward social consequences. David uses the same framing in Psalms 51:4: “Against you, you only, have I sinned.” Christ’s entire life is oriented God-ward: “I always do what pleases him” (John 8:29). The moral refusal that names the act as first a sin against God is the mark of the covenant-bearer’s character.
10: And though she spoke to Joseph day after day, he refused to go to bed with her or even to be with her — the repeated daily temptation that Joseph refuses day after day. The persistent temptation overcome by persistent refusal is the type of Christ’s complete sinlessness: “we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are — yet he did not sin” (Hebrews 4:15). The “in every way” encompasses the persistent, repeated nature of temptation: not a single great test but daily, relentless pressure. Joseph’s daily refusal — refusing “to go to bed with her or even to be with her” — shows the full scope of the avoidance: not merely the act but the occasion. Jesus flees the crowd’s attempt to make him king (John 6:15); he avoids the occasions of compromise as well as the acts.
11: One day he went into the house to attend to his duties, and none of the household servants was inside — the circumstances of isolation that the tempter exploits. Joseph goes about his ordinary duties; the occasion of vulnerability comes in the ordinary course of work, not in any unusual situation. The tempter exploits ordinary moments: “your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour” (1 Peter 5:8). The ordinary moment of isolation — “none of the household servants was inside” — is the occasion the enemy chooses. Christ was tempted in the wilderness, in an isolated place, apart from community. The isolation that makes temptation easier is not avoided by those who must do their daily work; it is faced and overcome.
12: She caught him by his cloak and said, “Come to bed with me!” But he left his cloak in her hand and ran out of the house — Joseph’s flight from temptation. He does not negotiate; he runs. The cloak left behind is the cost of the flight. Paul’s instruction: “Flee from sexual immorality” (1 Corinthians 6:18); “Flee the evil desires of youth” (2 Timothy 2:22). The cost of fleeing is the cloak — something is left behind, something is lost. But the loss of the cloak is nothing compared to the gain of integrity. Joseph’s flight is the type of the believer’s battle with temptation: not to stand and reason but to flee and leave behind what the tempter grasps at. The cloak that remains in the enemy’s hand will become the false evidence — but the one who fled is innocent.
13: When she saw that he had left his cloak in her hand and had run out of the house — the moment when the attempt at temptation becomes the occasion for false accusation. The tempter who is refused becomes the accuser. The structural parallel to Satan as “the accuser of our brothers and sisters, who accuses them before our God day and night” (Revelation 12:10) is embedded in this narrative: the one who tempted becomes the one who accuses. The refused temptation triggers the false charge. Christ was falsely accused by those who could not successfully tempt him: the religious leaders who failed to trap him with questions then used false witnesses at his trial (Matthew 26:59–61). Temptation failed → false accusation → unjust judgment: the pattern of the passion.
14: She called her household servants. “Look,” she said to them, “this Hebrew has been brought to us to make sport of us! He came in here to sleep with me, but I screamed” — the false accusation constructed from the abandoned cloak. The accusation inverts the truth entirely: the one who refused is accused of attempt; the one who was innocent is presented as guilty. The false witness that inverts the truth is what the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin used at Jesus’s trial: “They were looking for false testimony… but they did not find any” (Matthew 26:59–60, though eventually two false witnesses were found). The righteous one condemned by a construction of lies from physical evidence is the type of Christ’s trial.
15: “When he heard me scream for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house” — the false narrative built around the real cloak. The real evidence (the cloak) supports the false story: the physical truth becomes the carrier of the narrative lie. The passion narrative similarly uses real facts to support a false verdict: Jesus really was seen in the temple (the disciples confirm it); he really did speak of destroying and rebuilding the temple (John 2:19); these truths are twisted into “I am able to destroy the temple of God and rebuild it in three days” (Matthew 26:61) — a misrepresentation used as a false accusation. The real evidence in a false framing is the structure of the unjust accusation against both Joseph and Christ.
16: She kept his cloak beside her until his master came home — Potiphar’s wife keeps the cloak as preserved evidence. The maintained accusation — the deliberately preserved false evidence — is the type of the sustained hostility that awaited Jesus throughout his ministry: “the Pharisees went out and began to plot with the Herodians how they might kill Jesus” (Mark 3:6). The cloak kept as a weapon until the moment of confrontation is the type of the sustained accusation that precedes a show trial. The covenant-bearer who is innocent faces not a spontaneous accusation but a sustained, prepared one.
17: Then she told him this story: “That Hebrew slave you brought us came to me to make sport of me” — the false accusation delivered to Potiphar in the form of a narrative (“this story” — the text uses the word sipper, narrative/account). The constructed narrative that reframes the events in a false light is the type of the false testimony at Jesus’s trial: the events of the temple-cleansing and Jesus’s saying about the temple are woven into a false narrative (Matthew 26:61). The false narrative that harms the innocent is what Psalms 27:12 cries out against: “Do not turn me over to the desire of my foes, for false witnesses rise up against me, spouting malicious accusations.” Christ is the fulfillment of this Psalm in his trial.
18: “But as soon as I screamed for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house” — the false narrative’s conclusion. The accusation ends with the cloak as physical evidence — the one piece of objective evidence that seems to confirm the entire false story. The physical evidence that seems to confirm the false narrative: at Jesus’s arrest, the disciples flee (Matthew 26:56: “Then all the disciples deserted him and fled”); Jesus is alone among enemies, exactly as the accusers want. The physical facts of abandonment and isolation seem to confirm the accusation’s framing. The innocent one surrounded by seeming confirmation of the false charge is the Christ-type’s pattern.
19: When his master heard the story his wife told him, saying, “This is how your slave treated me,” he burned with anger — Potiphar’s anger at the false accusation. The righteous-seeming anger of the judge who has been deceived is the type of the anger directed at Jesus by those who misunderstand: “They all condemned him as worthy of death” (Mark 14:64). The judges who condemn with genuine anger based on false information: the Sanhedrin genuinely believed (or claimed to believe) Jesus was a blasphemer; Potiphar genuinely believed (or appeared to believe) his wife. The unjust verdict delivered with genuine-seeming outrage is the consistent context of the covenant-bearer’s unjust suffering.
20: Joseph’s master took him and put him in prison, the place where the king’s prisoners were confined. But while Joseph was there in the prison — the descent deepens: from the pit (Genesis 37:24, thrown in by brothers) to slavery to prison. Each descent appears to be the end; each descent is actually the preparation for the next level of the divine purpose. The pattern of descent-through-imprisonment leading to exaltation is the Joseph Christ-type at its most structural: Philippians 2:6–9 describes Christ’s descent (divine form → servant form → death → death on a cross) followed by ascent (exalted → name above every name → every knee bowing). Joseph’s pit → slavery → prison → exaltation as Pharaoh’s vizier is the OT’s most sustained narrative type of the Christ-descent-and-ascent pattern.
21: But the LORD was with him; he showed him unfailing love and granted him favor in the eyes of the prison warden — “The LORD was with him” — the third occurrence in this chapter (vv2, 3, 21). Even in prison, the divine presence continues. The divine presence in the worst place is one of the OT’s most important theological claims: Psalms 139:8: “If I make my bed in the depths, you are there.” The LORD shows Joseph hesed — covenant faithfulness, steadfast love — in the prison. Christ promises his presence specifically in the places of suffering: “For I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me, I was naked and you clothed me, I was sick and you visited me, I was in prison and you came to me” (Matthew 25:35–36). Christ identifies with the imprisoned. The LORD’s presence with Joseph in prison is the type of Christ’s solidarity with the suffering.
22: The warden put Joseph in charge of all those held in the prison, and he was made responsible for all that was done there — Joseph rises to authority even in prison. The same pattern as in Potiphar’s house (v4: “put him in charge of his household”): wherever Joseph is, he rises to stewardship through divine favor. The principle: “For whoever has will be given more, and they will have an abundance” (Matthew 25:29). The faithful steward who is trusted with more in each place is the type of Christ as the one to whom “all authority in heaven and on earth” is given (Matthew 28:18) — the ultimate exaltation of the one who proved faithful in every circumstance. Joseph in the prison is faithful; Christ in death is faithful (“into your hands I commit my spirit,” Luke 23:46) — and both are exalted.
23: The warden paid no attention to anything under Joseph’s care, because the LORD was with Joseph and gave him success in whatever he did — the fourth “the LORD was with Joseph” concludes the chapter. The chapter began with Joseph’s involuntary descent into Egypt as a slave (v1) and ends with Joseph as a trusted steward even in prison (v23). The divine presence transforms every circumstance — not by removing the suffering but by making the sufferer fruitful and faithful within it. Paul from prison: “I can do all this through him who gives me strength” (Philippians 4:13); “The Lord stood at my side and gave me strength” (2 Timothy 4:17). The divine presence that makes the prisoner successful in whatever he does is the same presence Christ promises to those he sends into the world: “And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age” (Matthew 28:20).
